exemplar.title: To what extent has the 2019 parking fee increase implemented by the municipality of Amsterdam been successful in decreasing the negative externality of traffic congestion?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the 2019 parking fee increase implemented by the municipality of Amsterdam been successful in decreasing the negative externality of traffic congestion?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is clearly stated using an evaluative stem and is consistently signposted and linked to analysis and conclusion.
The title is concise, discipline-specific and immediately frames the investigationâs focus and purpose.
The methodological approach employs an appropriate range of primary and secondary sources with clear evidence of informed selection.
weaknesses: The breadth of the question (three externalities, two policy measures) occasionally dilutes depth in sections like noise analysis.
Key methodological details of the primary survey (sampling frame, margin of error) remain under-specified, limiting rigour.
Some secondary sourcesâ data-collection methods are only briefly noted, affecting transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands (topic communication, research question focus, methodology) consistently meet the top band descriptors. Despite minor breadth and transparency issues, the work demonstrates accurate and effective communication of purpose with a fully appropriate range of methods and clear evidence of informed selection, warranting the maximum 6 marks.

strengths: Key economic concepts (MPB, MSC, welfare loss, opportunity cost) are employed correctly in diagrams and text.
Theory diagrams are accurately drawn, labelled and interpreted, demonstrating coherent understanding.
A broad array of municipal statistics and academic sources are integrated directly into the argument.
weaknesses: Multiple conceptual inaccuracies persist (misdefinition of demerit goods, conflation of MSC and MPC, mislabelling curves).
Some terminology is imprecise (e.g., calling a parking fee a price floor).
Source application is sometimes descriptive, lacking critical scrutiny of reliability or potential bias.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge with generally appropriate use of terminology and a wide range of sources, repeated conceptual errors and lapses in precision limit performance to the 3â4 band. The evidence of partially effective source application and terminology use supports a score of 4.

strengths: Research draws on an extensive mix of relevant municipal datasets, peer-reviewed studies and primary survey data directly linked to the research question.
Analysis integrates theory and empirical evidence, using charts and statistics to trace causal pathways from policy interventions to outcomes.
The discussion acknowledges trade-offs between different externalities and proposes evaluative recommendations (e.g., adjusting resident discounts).
weaknesses: Evaluation is uneven, with some claims asserted without sufficient evidence or quantitative support.
Counter-arguments and alternative explanations (e.g., factory emissions) lack econometric depth and critical appraisal.
Key analytical points (e.g., quantifying demand shifts, cost-benefit trade-offs) are described qualitatively rather than with explicit numerical estimates.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research relevance and analysis depth) clearly sit in the 7â9 band, while the discussion and evaluation strand falls in the 4â6 band due to uneven critical appraisal. On balance, the work demonstrates substantial appropriate and relevant research with mostly supported analysis but only moderate critical evaluation, justifying a mid-7 score.

strengths: The report follows a logical chapter structure with clear headings and consistent reference to figures and tables.
Major elements of IB EE presentation conventions are present (justified text, captioned figures).
weaknesses: The table of contents lacks uniform formatting and complete page numbers.
Page numbering is missing; citation and reference list formatting are inconsistent.
Caption and heading styles vary, and some images are low resolution, detracting from professionalism.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All presentation strands fall within the 1â2 band. While the structure is generally appropriate, multiple formatting and layout inconsistencies impede clarity, placing the work at the upper end of this band with a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Strengths.** The research question is explicitly worded, uses the evaluative stem âto what extent,â and is referred to in methodology, analysis and conclusion. Signposting in section titles (e.g., âMeasure 1â, âMeasure 2â) reinforces the direct link between evidence and the question.

**Weaknesses.** While focused, the question covers several different externalities and two distinct interventions, leading to occasional dilution of depth (e.g., brief treatment of noise data). This slight breadth prevents full precision.

The question is clear and effectively connected to the discussion but not perfectly delimited; therefore the strand sits convincingly in band 5-6, at a score of 5.

**Strengths.** The student states a concise, discipline-specific title and immediately articulates the purpose â assessing the effectiveness of Amsterdamâs anti-car measures in reducing negative externalities. The introduction and section headings keep this focus visible throughout, and the analysis repeatedly links back to congestion, pollution and noise. The topic is contemporary, locally grounded and clearly suitable for a systematic economic investigation.

**Weaknesses.** Scope is broad (three externalities, two major policies), which occasionally leads to descriptive passages and some overlap between sections, but the overall communication of purpose remains clear.

Given the consistently clear articulation of topic, purpose and focus, the work best fits the top band (5-6); the minor breadth issue merits the lower mark in that band (6).

**Strengths.** The essay draws on a broad array of municipal statistics, peer-reviewed journal articles and policy reports, integrating them directly into argument (e.g., Ostermeijer et al. 2022 for parking data; Statista COâ figures). Sources clearly illuminate each policyâs effects, and visual evidence (maps, charts) is woven into the text.

**Weaknesses.** Some applications remain descriptive, and at times data are cited without critical scrutiny of reliability (e.g., survey limitations, potential confounding factors for pollution trends). A few claims rely heavily on single secondary sources.

Given the largely pertinent and well-applied evidence, but with occasional lapses in critical use, the strand fits solidly in band 5-6, earning 5.

**Strengths.** The report follows IB EE conventions (abstract omitted per new guide), with logical chapters, sub-headings and consistent use of figures and tables that aid navigation.

**Weaknesses.** Table of contents lacks full page numbers; appendices and some headings are inconsistently styled; occasional mid-quote breaks disrupt flow. The structure is therefore only âgenerally appropriate.â

These characteristics place the work in band 1-2 for structure appropriateness; the upper value 2 reflects that most major elements are present despite inconsistencies.

**Strengths.** Basic layout conventions (title, word count, justified text) are present. Figures are captioned and referenced.

**Weaknesses.** Page numbers are missing; table cells are mis-aligned; caption format varies; several images are blurred. These shortcomings impede professional presentation.

Given multiple layout inaccuracies, the strand fits the lower end of band 1-2; score 1 is warranted.

**Strengths.** The essay moves beyond description by weighing successes (congestion) against limitations (air/noise pollution) and considering alternative explanations (factory emissions, illegal exhausts). Recommendations to tighten resident discounts show evaluative thinking.

**Weaknesses.** Evaluation is uneven: some claims are asserted rather than evidenced, and the conclusion does not quantify âto what extentâ with clear criteria. Logical flow occasionally drifts into narrative rather than analysis.

This balanced but partially critical discussion accords with band 4-6; mid-band score 5 is appropriate.

**Strengths.** The student integrates theory with empirical evidence, using diagrams, survey percentages and municipal data to trace causal pathways from policy to outcomes. Multiple externalities are analysed, and conclusions generally follow from evidence (e.g., congestion reduction linked to 17 % fall in parking-hours).

**Weaknesses.** Quantification is sometimes qualitative (statements like âsignificantly reducedâ without explicit figures), and counter-arguments on pollution/noise lack econometric depth. Some diagram explanations (leftward vs rightward demand shifts) contain minor inconsistencies.

The analysis is clear and mostly supported, matching the 7-9 band; moderate depth justifies a score of 7.

**Strengths.** The student demonstrates clear understanding of negative externalities, welfare loss, and policy tools (parking fees as price instruments, speed limits affecting opportunity cost). Theory diagrams are correctly drawn and interpreted.

**Weaknesses.** An inaccurate definition of demerit goods (âlead to a rise in societal welfareâ) reveals conceptual confusion; some explanations (e.g., price floor vs. parking fee) are imprecise. Occasional conflation of MSC with MPC suggests partial misunderstanding.

Overall knowledge is coherent but blemished by notable inaccuracies; this aligns with band 3-4, at the upper end (4).

**Strengths.** The investigation employs an extensive mix of municipal datasets, peer-reviewed studies and a primary resident survey, each directly linked to aspects of the research question. Most sources are recent and clearly pertinent to Amsterdamâs policy context, demonstrating deliberate selection.

**Weaknesses.** A handful of sources (e.g., Glassdoor list of manufacturers) are tangential, and the reliability of some statistics is accepted without critique. Nevertheless, relevance remains high overall.

This breadth, currency and thematic alignment fulfil the 7-9 band; limited critical appraisal places it at 8 rather than higher.

**Strengths.** The student employs an appropriate mix of primary (survey of 41 residents) and secondary sources (municipal data, academic studies, Statista, etc.). Methods are explained, and data are selected purposefully to match each policy area. Diagrams are constructed to operationalise theory.

**Weaknesses.** Key details of the primary research are thin: sampling frame, questionnaire design and margin of error are not fully specified, and the modest sample size limits representativeness. Some secondary sourcesâ data-collection methods are only briefly noted.

Overall, the range is appropriate and selection appears informed, meeting band 5-6 descriptors; limitations in rigour justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

**Strengths.** Key economic termsâMPB, MSC, welfare loss, opportunity costâare employed frequently and generally correctly. Diagrams are labelled and referenced, indicating awareness of standard conceptual tools.

**Weaknesses.** Misstatement of âdemerit goodsâ and occasional misuse of âprice floorâ for parking fees indicate lapses in precision. Some terms (e.g., external marginal cost) could be deployed to deepen analysis but are absent.

Consistent but not flawless usage fits band 3-4; a score of 4 recognises overall accuracy with some errors.

annotations.comment: The title is concise, discipline-specific and immediately signals the investigationâs focus on Amsterdamâs anti-car measures and negative externalities, framing a clear purpose for a systematic economic analysis.
The table of contents shows inconsistent indentation and dot-leader formatting, which detracts from professional presentation. Align entries and leaders uniformly.
The survey-derived pie chart lacks discussion of sampling error and confidence intervals. Address representativeness to validate conclusions.
Integrating primary survey data (71% agreement) effectively triangulates with secondary findings on congestion reduction, enhancing overall research relevance.
Citing the Quality of Life Index without critiquing its methodology limits depth. Discuss index components and relevance to this study.
Page numbers are missing throughout the report, which impedes navigation and reference. Ensure all pages are numbered consistently in the header or footer.
The research question uses the evaluative stem âto what extent,â clearly linking it to subsequent methodology, analysis and conclusion and establishing a strong focus for investigation.
The sentence âIn this case, the externalities will beâ is left incomplete. Ensure all definitions are fully articulated to avoid confusion.
Describing parking fees as a âprice floorâ is conceptually inaccurate; this policy is a tax, not a mandated minimum price. Revise terminology for precision.
Figure 1 is introduced without further analysis linking specific map features to policy impacts. Integrate description of parking areas and traffic patterns into your economic evaluation.
The research question is repeated mid-paragraph, causing fragmentation. Consolidate its presentation in the introduction for smoother flow.
The primary surveyâs sampling frame and questionnaire design are under-specified. Provide details on sampling method, margin of error and question construction to substantiate representativeness.
The discussion of secondary sources describes reliability but lacks critical scrutiny of potential bias or data-collection limitations. Evaluate source strengths and weaknesses explicitly.
The definition of demerit goods is incorrect; they decrease societal welfare when over-consumed. Correct the concept to avoid fundamental misunderstanding.
Figure 2 labels MSC as MPC, conflating private and social cost. Ensure correct curve identification to accurately represent welfare loss.
The shift from D1 to D2 is described qualitatively; include explicit numerical estimates (e.g., change in Q) to deepen analysis.
Section heading contains an errant period â30km/h. On the costâ. Review formatting to maintain consistent heading style.
Figure 5 lacks data for the magnitude of the D2âD3 shift. Provide empirical estimates or survey results to support diagrammatic claims.
The cumulative survey result (68% find car use difficult) is presented without restating sample size or margin of error here. Reinforce methodological transparency in each section.
Discussion lacks cost-benefit weighing of short-term resident costs versus longer-term gains. Expand evaluation to assess trade-offs critically.
Figure 7 map comparison is descriptive; strengthen analysis by quantifying changes in spatial distribution of parking prices with statistical measures.
Relying on a Glassdoor list of manufacturers to explain air pollution is tangential. Critically select sources directly linked to emissions data.
âI donât knowâ responses (7%) are not discussed. Evaluate uncertainty and its implications for policy assessment.
Figure 9 shows mixed views on alternative transport. Analyze neutral and disagree segments to offer a balanced interpretation of policy impact.
The conclusion reiterates results but does not quantify âto what extentâ outcomes; integrate clear metrics or thresholds to answer the research question definitively.
Reflection on research limitations and decision-making is absent. Critically evaluate how challenges shaped methodology and future research directions.
Reference list formatting is inconsistent (varying bullet symbols and title capitalization). Standardize citation style to improve presentation.
